hero.title: Turn any image into a ready-to-use AI prompt
hero.subtitle: Free library plus Chrome extension: get a structured image-to-prompt draft from any reference—subject, light, palette, composition—then paste into Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, or your favorite model.

heroExtension.title: Image To Prompt for Chrome
heroExtension.subtitle: Copy prompts from images as you browse—structured language grounded in the reference, synced with your ImagePrompt library.

problem.title: Great AI art starts with a prompt that actually describes the scene
problem.b1: Strong prompts spell out the hero subject, environment, and art direction—yet most people only remember a few adjectives when the tab is gone.
problem.b2: Guessing light direction, palette, lens feel, and mood burns time; models return almost-right frames until you luck into the magic phrase.
problem.b3: Manual screenshots and copy/paste across tools add friction right when you should be iterating on the idea—not the window layout.

accuracy.title: Highly accurate image-to-prompt generation
accuracy.subtitle: Compare a reference with a prompt-aware regeneration to see how tightly the language tracks what you liked. Results vary by model and settings—treat it as a sanity check, not a guarantee.
accuracy.descA: Prompt emphasizes subject separation, stormy cool palette, and metallic speculars—useful when you want the model to echo mood and material, not just composition.
accuracy.descB: A warmer, slightly softer read of the same frame—shows how small language shifts steer color grading and micro-contrast without changing the scene layout.
accuracy.descC: Higher clarity bias in the prompt family: edges read crisper, shadows a touch deeper—handy when your target model tends to mush fine detail.
accuracy.descD: More diffuse key-light wording in the extract—foreground feels gentler while background structure stays anchored; good for portrait-leaning generators.

testimonials.title: What our users say
testimonials.subtitle: Short notes from people who live in reference boards, mood films, and iteration-heavy AI workflows.
testimonials.mayaQuote: I stopped screenshotting Pinterest into Notes. The overlay hands me vocabulary I would've forgotten—lighting, texture, palette—in one pass.
testimonials.jonasQuote: My first draft prompts used to be 'cinematic, 8k, detailed.' Now I start from what the reference actually does with color and composition.
testimonials.elenaQuote: The structured breakdown reads like a shot list. I tweak two chips, paste into my generator, and I'm already closer than hours of trial and error.
testimonials.chrisQuote: We share references in Slack all day. Image to Prompt is the fastest way to turn a link into language the team can reuse across tools.
testimonials.amiraQuote: It's not magic—it still needs judgment—but it cut our 'almost right' loops way down for campaign visuals.
testimonials.tomQuote: Free tier is enough to learn how prompts map to what I see. When I need more runs, pricing is clear.

how.title: How to use Image to Prompt
how.subtitle: From install to a prompt you can ship—four quick steps, no tab circus.
how.step1Body: Add Image To Prompt from the Chrome Web Store and pin it for one-click access.
how.step2Body: On feeds, shops, or articles—open the overlay when the frame matches the look you want.
how.step3Body: Get structured language, then refine in Image to prompt or paste into another image model.
how.step4Body: Edit keywords, rerun, or use your own photo—the same extract → refine loop every time.
how.promptSnippet: A close-up portrait with soft window light, warm skin tones, shallow depth of field, natural texture in fabric and hair, editorial color grade, calm confident expression.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Image prompts, Chrome workflow, and how Image to prompt fits your AI art stack—plain-language answers.
faq.a1: It is the written brief you give an AI image model: who or what is in frame, where the scene happens, which stylistic lane you want, and how light and color should feel. The clearer that brief, the less guesswork the model needs.
faq.q2: Why does prompt quality matter for AI photos?
faq.a2: Models follow statistical patterns from your words. Vague language spreads probability across many looks; specific cues for camera, materials, and atmosphere narrow the distribution so outputs land nearer to the picture in your head.
faq.q3: What should a strong prompt include?
faq.a3: Most effective prompts blend a clear subject, environment or backdrop, palette or grading notes, lighting direction, lens or format hints when relevant, and stylistic references. Image To Prompt is built to surface those layers from a reference instead of asking you to invent them from memory.
faq.q4: What does Image To Prompt do in the browser?
faq.a4: It reads the reference image you are looking at and drafts structured language—light, palette, composition, texture, stylistic signals—so you copy a starting brief rather than a handful of generic adjectives.
faq.q5: Where does the Chrome extension work?
faq.a5: Any site where you can hover an image: inspiration grids, social feeds, editorials, shops, and galleries. Open the overlay on the frame you care about, then continue in Image to prompt when you want the full extract → expand path or generation.
faq.q6: Do I need an Image to prompt account?
faq.a6: Lightweight browsing and copying a draft may work without signing in. Running the full upload → extract → expand pipeline and on-site generation expects an active session (cookies on imageprompt.tools), matching the web panel experience.
faq.q7: Can I paste the prompt into other AI image tools?
faq.a7: Yes. Treat the text as a starting point. Different engines prefer different emphasis—some react strongly to camera tokens, others to painterly adjectives—so expect to tweak wording to match the generator you use.
faq.q8: Do prompts behave the same in every AI image generator?
faq.a8: The fundamentals overlap, but each platform has its own sensitivities: token limits, banned terms, and which descriptors move the needle. Image To Prompt gives you a rich baseline; fine-tune per tool the way you would adapt a creative brief for different studios.
faq.q9: How does Image to prompt handle privacy for uploads?
faq.a9: The extension activates when you use it—there is no always-on scraping of every page. For what the site stores, how long media is retained, and third parties, read our <privacy>Privacy</privacy> policy and keep sensitive references off shared machines.
faq.q10: Will my output look identical to the reference?
faq.a10: No honest tool promises a pixel-perfect clone. Model choice, seed, safety filters, and your own source photo all move the result. The goal here is a stronger first prompt so you spend fewer cycles chasing the same vibe.

Privacy.title: Privacy policy
Privacy.draftNote: This English text is a draft for review and should be confirmed with legal counsel where needed.
Privacy.s1p1: This Privacy Policy describes how we collect, store, process, and protect personal data of users of the website {domain} (the “Service”, “{site}”).
Privacy.s1p2: By using the Service you agree to this Policy. If you do not agree, please stop using the Service.
Privacy.s2li1: <bold>Sign-in data:</bold> When you sign in with Google we receive identifiers and profile information provided by the identity provider (for example account ID and display name). We do not receive your password.
Privacy.s2li2: <bold>Usage data:</bold> Pages viewed, saved prompts, and generations you create. This helps us improve the Service.
Privacy.s2li3: <bold>Technical data:</bold> IP address, browser type, operating system, and referral source (including UTM parameters), collected automatically for analytics.
Privacy.s2li4: <bold>Cookies:</bold> Used for authentication and analytics (e.g. Yandex Metrica).
Privacy.s3li1: Operating the Service and authenticating users
Privacy.s3li3: Analyzing usage and improving the Service
Privacy.s4p1: Data is stored on servers located in the Russian Federation and the European Union. We apply standard safeguards against unauthorized access, alteration, or destruction.
Privacy.s4p2: We retain data for as long as you use the Service. After account deletion, data is removed within 30 days unless a longer period is required by law.
Privacy.s5intro: We do not sell your personal data. We may share data only as follows:
Privacy.s5li1: <bold>Analytics:</bold> Yandex Metrica for aggregated traffic statistics.
Privacy.s5li2: <bold>Distribution platforms:</bold> Public prompt cards may appear on Pinterest or social networks as public content, not as personal data.
Privacy.s5li3: <bold>Legal requirements:</bold> When required by applicable law.
Privacy.s6li1: Request information about data we hold
Privacy.s6li4: Withdraw consent to processing where applicable
Privacy.s6contact: To exercise these rights, contact us at <mail>{emailAddress}</mail>.
Privacy.s7p1: We may update this Policy. The current version is always available at <privacyLink>{privacyUrl}</privacyLink>. Continued use after changes means you accept the updated Policy.
Privacy.s8p1: For questions about personal data processing: <mail>{emailAddress}</mail>.

BlogAiPromptExamples.h1: AI image prompt examples that actually work
BlogAiPromptExamples.intro: Strong AI image prompts read like a tiny creative brief: subject, environment, light, color, lens or format, and style. Weak prompts stop at “cinematic, 8k, detailed”—they give the model too many equally likely directions.
BlogAiPromptExamples.structureP: Start with who or what owns the frame, then place them in a setting. Add one or two lighting sentences (direction, softness, color temperature). Name materials and textures when they matter. Close with stylistic guardrails—film stock, painterly vs photographic, era—so the model does not drift.
BlogAiPromptExamples.beforeAfterP: Before: a bag of adjectives. After: a short stack of concrete cues tied to composition and mood. You can still iterate; you are just iterating from a prompt that already encodes the scene you liked.
BlogAiPromptExamples.h2_tool: Image → prompt as a starting point
BlogAiPromptExamples.toolP: When you have a reference but not the words, image-to-prompt turns pixels into language you can edit. Paste the draft into Midjourney, SDXL, or any tool you use—then adapt tokens to what that engine respects.
